coredata.dc:title: Electronic and optical properties of antimonene/palladium ditelluride (Sb/PdTe2) heterostructure with the effect of strain and external electric field: A computational study
coredata.dc:creator: Shokri, Asiye
Yazdani, Ahmad
Rahimi, Kourosh
coredata.dc:description: In this article, the structural, electronic, and optical properties of antimonene/palladium ditelluride van der Waals heterostructure were investigated using density functional theory. Our results revealed the Sb/PdTe2 heterostructure in its most stable configuration is an indirect semiconductor with energy bandgap of 0.49 eV and a type-I band alignment. We also studied the impact of strain and external electric field on the structural and electronic properties of this heterostructure. Our results indicate that the band alignment of this heterostructure could be controlled by changing the external electric field and strain parameters to make type-I or type-II semiconductors or a conductor with energy bandgap between 0 eV and 0.55 eV. Furthermore, the calculated optical properties of Sb/PdTe2 show that the heterostructure absorbs light in ultraviolet and visible regions. Overall, it is expected that the novel constructed Sb/PdTe2 heterostructure be a good candidate for electronic and optoelectronic applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Antimonene
Palladium ditelluride
vdW heterostructure
Optoelectronics
Band alignment
Density functional theory

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body mmlmath acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantsponsor grantsponsorid orcid primabst ref

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Top and side views of (a) PdTe2 and (b) Sb monolayers. Band structures of (c) PdTe2 and (d) Sb monolayers, respectively, at the HSE06 level.
Top and side views of four different possible configurations to create the Sb/PdTe2 heterostructure. The most stable (lowest-energy) one is C3.

(a) Layer-projected band structure of the Sb/PdTe2 heterostructure, showing a type-I indirect bandgap semiconductor, where VBM and CBM levels are mainly contributed by PdTe2. (b) A schematic representation of the band alignment in the heterostructure.

(a) Plane-averaged electrostatic potential drop across the interface of the Sb/PdTe2 heterostructure. (b) Isosurface of the charge density difference, where yellow and cyan indicate gain and loss of electrons, respectively. (c) The amount of charge transfer from PdTe2 to Sb layers. (d) Charge density difference across the interface of the Sb/PdTe2 heterostructure. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Layer-projected band structure for (a) Eext = −1.5 V/Å, (b) Eext = 0 V/Å, and (c) Eext = +1.25 V/Å. (d) Energy bandgaps for different external electric fields. (e) VBM and CBM levels for different external electric fields.
(a) Layer-projected band structures, (b) energy bandgaps, and (c) VBM and CBM levels of the heterostructure for different compressive strains.

Absorption coefficient spectra of the individual Sb and PdTe2 monolayers as well as the Sb/PdTe2 heterostructure calculated at the HSE06 level. The incident AM 1.5G solar flux spectrum (ASTM G173-03 Reference Spectra) is also provided as a reference.

Lattice constant (a), bond length (b), buckling parameter (h), the inter-layer distance (d), energy bandgaps (Eg) for GGA and HSE06 calculations, and total energy of four different possible configurations.
The effect of different external electric fields on the lattice constant (a), bond length (b), interlayer distance (d), buckling parameter (h), and bandgap Eg(HSE06) of the Sb/PdTe2 heterostructure.
The effect of different biaxial strains on the lattice constant (a), bond length (b), inter-layer distance (d), buckling parameter (h), and bandgap Eg(HSE06) of the Sb/PdTe2 heterostructure.

head.ce:title: Electronic and optical properties of antimonene/palladium ditelluride (Sb/PdTe2) heterostructure with the effect of strain and external electric field: A computational study

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Condensed Matter Physics Group, Department of Basic Sciences, Tarbiat Modares University, Jalal-Ale-Ahmad Avenue, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Condensed Matter Physics Group, Department of Basic Sciences, Tarbiat Modares University, Jalal-Ale-Ahmad Avenue, Tehran, Iran

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Condensed Matter Physics Group, Department of Basic Science, Tarbiat Modares University, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this article, the structural, electronic, and optical properties of antimonene/palladium ditelluride van der Waals heterostructure were investigated using density functional theory. Our results revealed the Sb/PdTe2 heterostructure in its most stable configuration is an indirect semiconductor with energy bandgap of 0.49 eV and a type-I band alignment. We also studied the impact of strain and external electric field on the structural and electronic properties of this heterostructure. Our results indicate that the band alignment of this heterostructure could be controlled by changing the external electric field and strain parameters to make type-I or type-II semiconductors or a conductor with energy bandgap between 0 eV and 0.55 eV. Furthermore, the calculated optical properties of Sb/PdTe2 show that the heterostructure absorbs light in ultraviolet and visible regions. Overall, it is expected that the novel constructed Sb/PdTe2 heterostructure be a good candidate for electronic and optoelectronic applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Since graphene is limited in the semiconductor field because of the absence of an electronic bandgap, scientists are constantly trying to discover and explore other two-dimensional materials especially those with a bandgap. Currently, a wide variety of two-dimensional materials have been produced from metal to insulator, especially semiconductors with unique properties, which are of great importance for electronic and optoelectronic devices due to their finite bandgaps [1–5].

There are some methods to tune the electronic and optical properties of pure semiconductors, which are mainly doping [6–8] and inducing defects [9]. Moreover, pure semiconductors typically exhibit inefficient optoelectronic properties due to their extremely high e-h recombination rate, which is due to the largely overlapping wave functions of the generated electrons and holes. A popular approach to reducing the high e-h recombination rate of a pure semiconductor and tuning its energy bandgap, bandgap type, and band alignment is by constructing heterojunctions and heterostructures of it to achieve different band alignment types including type-I (straddling), type-II (staggered), and type-III (broken). Such heterojunctions can be seen in a variety of applications [10–16]. Recently, such heterostructures can also be seen in the field of 2D materials by stacking two different monolayers to create vdW heterostructures to modify them and achieve their applications in novel electronic and optical devices [17–22].

One of the most important two-dimensional semiconductors from the group-VA is the graphene-like honeycomb wide-bandgap antimonene (Sb monolayer) with its naturally layered β phase. Unlike its bulk phase, the Sb monolayer is predicted to be a semiconductor with an indirect bandgap of 2.28 eV [23], which undergoes an indirect-to-direct bandgap transition by applying tensile strains. This material with its unique properties analogous to black phosphorene has recently attracted theoretical and experimental researchers due to its wide bandgap, favorable stability, and high carrier mobility [24]. Singh et al. investigated the optical properties of antimonene and found it an appropriate material for ultraviolet optical nanodevices and solar cells [25]. After the prediction of the stability of this monolayer, different experimental methods were utilized to find the most effective way to synthesize the antimonene monolayer [26–32]. Besides, several vdW heterostructures have been proposed and studied using antimonene, including antimonene/graphene [33], antimonene/bismuthene [34], and antimonene/germanene [35], etc.

Transition metal dichalcogenides (TMDs) are an interesting group of 2D materials that have been used to create heterostructures. There are more than 40 bulk TMDs with the MX2 formula (M is a transition metal like Mo, W, Pd, Pt, etc. and X is a chalcogen like S, Se, or Te) with a three-layer structure, where a transition metal layer is sandwiched between two chalcogen layers. Due to their various electronic properties, from metals to insulators, like superconductivity, charge density wave, sizable bandgap, and indirect-to-direct bandgap transition, TMDs are good candidates for electronic and optoelectronic applications [36–42]. In 2017, Wu et al. chose PdTe2 as a substrate to grow Sb monolayer (antimonene) by molecular beam epitaxy technique because of the very small mismatch of the lattice constants of the two layers and its chemical stability as a layered transition metal dichalcogenide [28]. Therefore, it is interesting to have a computational view on how the electric field and strain can affect the electronic and optical properties of such heterostructure.

We recently investigated the electronic and optical properties of the Sb/PtTe2 vdW heterostructure using first-principles calculations [43]. In the present work, we designed the Sb/PdTe2 vdW heterostructure to investigate its electronic and optical properties and the effects of strain and external electric fields on the bandgap and the band alignment of the Sb/PdTe2 heterostructure. Our results indicated that this heterostructure has an indirect bandgap with a type-I band alignment, while biaxial strains and external electric fields could tune the band alignment. We also studied the optical properties of Sb/PdTe2, showing an enhanced light absorption, particularly in UV and visible regions.

Our calculations were performed using density functional theory with a plane-wave basis set and optimized norm-conserving Vanderbilt pseudopotentials [44] with the generalized gradient approximation given by Perdew, Burke, and Ernzerhof (PBE) [45] for exchange-correlation potential, as implemented in Quantum Espresso [46]. Kinetic-energy cutoffs of 80 and 640 Ry were used for expansions of electronic wave functions and charge densities, respectively. The Monkhorst-Pack scheme was adopted for k-point sampling of Brillouin zone integrations with a 15 × 15 × 1 k-mesh, and a large vacuum space of 20 Å was used along the z-direction to avoid unphysical interactions between individual pictures. The convergence criterion for energy in SCF cycles was chosen to be 10−6 Ry. The geometric structures were optimized by minimizing the forces on individual atoms with the criterion that the total force on each atom must be below 10−3 Ry/Bohr. The vdW-DF method was used to describe the vdW interactions between the individual monolayers [47–49]. The pure DFT is usually not able to correctly estimate the bandgap of semiconductors. There are several known methods to compensate for the underestimation, including the addition of Hubbard U term, the screened-exchange local-density approximation, and using hybrid functional like HSE06 [50,51]. Here, we calculated the electronic properties using the HSE06 (Heyd–Scuseria–Ernzerhof) hybrid functional [52]. Finally, the Wannier90 package [53] was employed to acquire maximally localized Wannier functions and obtain HSE06 band structures.

To study the Sb/PdTe2 vdW heterostructure, we first investigated the structural and electronic properties of the Sb and PdTe2 monolayers. After relaxation calculations, the lattice constants of Sb and PdTe2 were obtained as 4.15 and 4.06 Å, respectively. Top and Side views of the equilibrium geometries of Sb and PdTe2 monolayers and their band structures calculated using the HSE06 hybrid functional are shown in Fig. 1 , showing that both monolayers are indirect semiconductors with valence band maximum (VBM) levels located at the Γ point and conduction band maximum (CBM) levels located between Γ and M points. The bandgaps of antimonene were calculated 1.22 and 1.93 eV using GGA and HSE06 hybrid functional, respectively. The values are nearly close to the experimentally reported bandgap up to 1.44 eV [54]. The bandgaps of the PdTe2 monolayer were calculated 0.25 and 0.76 eV, which are close to other reports [55].

To create the Sb/PdTe2 vdW heterostructure, we applied a 1.05% lattice mismatch between the optimized PdTe2 and Sb monolayers. Hence, there was no significant effect on the electronic structures of the Sb and PdTe2 layers in the heterostructure due to such small strain. As it is shown in Fig. 1, we created four different possible configurations for the heterostructure to find the most stable one. The most stable configuration is when the lower Sb atom is located on top of the lower Te atom and the upper Sb atom is located on top of the Pd atom (Fig. 2 , c).

Additionally, we used the PBE (GGA) functional coupled with the van der Waals density functional (vdW-DF) to correctly account for the effect of vdW interactions. Our calculations show that the equilibrium distance between Sb and PdTe2 monolayers is 3.49 Å. To specify the stability of the Sb/PdTe2 heterostructure, we calculated the binding energy using the equation Eb = Eheterostructure - EPdTe2 - ESb, where Ehetero, EPdTe2, and ESb are the total energies of the Sb/PdTe2 heterostructure, the PdTe2 monolayer, and the Sb monolayer, respectively. We found that the vdW-corrected binding energy (−0.1780314 eV) was lower than the GGA binding energy (−0.0821436 eV), which is an indication of the vdW nature of the coupling between the individual layers.

Bandgaps of the individual monolayers and the heterostructure were first calculated using PBE, which resulted in the indirect bandgap of 1.55 eV for the Sb monolayer and the indirect bandgap of 0.25 eV for the PdTe2 monolayer, while the heterostructure became a metal. Therefore, we employed also the HSE06 hybrid functional to obtain better estimations for the bandgaps. The HSE06-calculated bandgaps were obtained 1.93, 0.76, and 0.49 eV for the Sb monolayer, the PdTe2 monolayer, and the Sb/PdTe2 heterostructure, respectively. Like the individual monolayers, the VBM and CBM levels of the heterostructure were also located at Γ-point and between Γ and M points, respectively. Fig. 3 shows the layer-projected band structure of Sb/PdTe2 using HSE06 functional. As it is clear, the VBM and CBM levels of the heterostructure are both contributed by PdTe2, making a type-I band alignment.

Table 1 represents the lattice constant (a), the bond length (b), the buckling parameter (h), the inter-layer distance between the two monolayers in the heterostructure (d), bandgaps (Eg) for GGA and HSE06 calculations, and total energies of four different possible configurations for the heterostructures. According to the total energies, the most stable configuration is the C3 one.

The calculated electrostatic potential drop (0.69 eV) is shown in Fig. 4 (a), which indicates a deeper potential for the Sb monolayer than for the PdTe2 monolayer in the Sb/PdTe2 heterostructure, resulting in a built-in electric field from Sb to PdTe2 that increases the photo-generated carriers lifetime. We also investigated the charge transfer between Sb and PdTe2 layers by plotting differential charge density (Fig. 4 (b and d)), using the equation below along the z-direction:
(1)
Δ ρ
( z )
= ∫
ρ
S b / P d T e 2
d x d y −
∫
ρ
S b
d x d y − ∫
ρ
P d T e 2
d x d y
where
ρ
S b / P d T e 2
,
ρ
S b
, and
ρ
P d T e 2
are charge densities of the heterostructure, the Sb monolayer, and the PdTe2 monolayer, respectively. According to the isosurface of the differential charge density (Fig. 4(b)), the cyan and yellow colors indicate gain and loss of electrons, respectively. Accordingly, the PdTe2 monolayer gives electrons to the Sb monolayer leading to n-doping in Sb and p-doping in PdTe2. The amount of total electrons transferred from PdTe2 to Sb was calculated by:
(2)
Δ Q
( z )
=
∫
− ∞
z
ρ z
d z
The total charge transfer was found ~0.112 |e| per unit cell from PdTe2 to Sb, as shown in Fig. 4,c.

To investigate the tunability of this vdW heterostructure, we considered external electric fields in the range of −1.5 V/Å to +1.25 V/Å. Table 2 shows the effect of different external electric fields on the structural and electronic parameters of the Sb/PdTe2 heterostructure. As it is shown in Fig. 5 (a–c), Eext = −1.5 V/Å (+1.25 V/Å) results in a type-II band alignment with an energy bandgap of 0.16 eV (0.22 eV). Fig. 5 (d and e) indicates the energy bandgap and CBM and VBM levels for different external electric fields. As can be seen, for Eext = −1.5 V/Å, the CBM level is contributed by the Sb layer, while for Eext = +1.25 V/Å, the CBM level is contributed by PdTe2 and the VBM level is contributed by Sb.

To evaluate the efficiency of the heterostructure for optoelectronic applications, we calculated the optical absorption coefficient α(ω) of the individual Sb and PdTe2 monolayers as well as the Sb/PdTe2 heterostructure using the independent-particle picture with the following equation [56]:
(3)
α
( ω )
=
2
ω c
[
ε 1 2
( ω )
+
ε 2 2
( ω )
−
ε 1
( ω )
]
1 2
where ε 1(ω) and ε 2(ω) are the real and imaginary parts of the dielectric function. Using the Kramers-Kronig transformation, one can obtain ε 1(ω) from ε 2(ω) and calculate ε 2(ω) by summing over states using the following equation [57]:
(4)
ε 2
( ω )
=
4
π 2
e 2
Ω
lim
q → 0
1
q 2
∑
c . ν . k
2
ω k
δ
(
E
c k
−
E
ν k
− ω
)
×
μ
c k
+
e
α q
|
μ
ν k
μ
c k
+
e
β q
|
μ
ν k
∗
where Ω refers to the volume of the primitive cell, q refers to the electron momentum operator, ωk is the weight of the k point, and c and ν refer to the conduction and valence band states. The Exk is the corresponding eigenvalue and μxk is the corresponding wave function at the k point, respectively. Also, eα and eβ refer to the unit vectors for the three Cartesian directions [58]. According to Fig. 7 , it is clear that the Sb/PdTe2 heterostructure shows an extended absorption band as compared with those of individual Sb and PdTe2 monolayers. One can see that the charge transfer and the interlayer coupling between these individual monolayers induced overlapping electronic states that result in new optical transitions [56], causing enhanced absorption energy, particularly in the range of 300–800 nm.

In summary, by carrying out density functional theory calculation with the vdW correction, we investigated the structural, electronic, and optical properties of the Sb/PdTe2 heterostructure. Our results indicated that the Sb/PdTe2 heterostructure is a vdW heterostructure with electrostatic interactions and the binding energy of −0.178 eV. To compensate for the underestimation of bandgaps due to GGA, we employed the hybrid functional of HSE06. The most stable configured heterostructure showed an indirect narrow bandgap semiconductor with a type-I band alignment. We also demonstrated the tunability of the electronic properties of this heterostructure using external electric fields and compressive strains. Our calculations showed that the tensile strain of 1% causes a semiconductor-to-conductor transition, while compressive strains result in a type-II band alignment with a shift in the position of the CBM level. Furthermore, external electric fields resulted in a type-II band alignment with a narrower bandgap (in particular, Eg = 0.16 and 0.22 eV for Eext = −1.5 and + 1.25 V/Ǻ, respectively). Besides, the Sb/PdTe2 heterostructure exhibited an enhanced optical absorption especially in the visible region of light. These results can make the Sb/PdTe2 vdW heterostructure a promising material for photoelectric devices.

Asiye Shokri: Design and implementation of the research, the analysis of the results and the writing of the manuscript. Ahmad Yazdani: Supervision. Kourosh Rahimi: help in preparing and editing the revised manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The computations were performed at the computational physics laboratory of department of basic science at Tarbiat Modares University (TMU). The financial support by the research council of TMU is gratefully acknowledged. The authors declare no competing interests. The raw/processed data required to reproduce these findings are available from the corresponding author on reasonable requests.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent advances in two-dimensional materials beyond graphene
